perturbation.original_text: Heritage and Jasper will comply with all laws and regulations applicable to production of the Products, including without limitation, the laws and regulations of the United States Food and Drug Administration ("FDA"), United States Public Health Service ("PHS"), and any and all other applicable federal, state and local laws and regulations. Heritage warrants that the Products shall be released free from defects in workmanship and shall be manufactured in accordance with this Agreement and 21 C.F.R. Part 110 which is entitled "Current Good Manufacturing Practice in Manufacturing, Packing or Holding Human Food" and as it may be amended from time-to-time.
perturbation.changed_text: Heritage and Jasper will endeavor to comply with most laws and regulations applicable to production of the Products, including without limitation, the laws and regulations of the United States Food and Drug Administration ("FDA"), United States Public Health Service ("PHS"), and any and all other applicable federal, state and local laws and regulations. Heritage warrants that the Products shall be released free from defects in workmanship and may be manufactured in accordance with this Agreement and 21 C.F.R. Part 110 which is entitled "Current Good Manufacturing Practice in Manufacturing, Packing or Holding Human Food" and as it may be amended from time-to-time.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will comply' to 'will endeavor to comply' and 'shall be manufactured' to 'may be manufactured' weakens the obligation to adhere to FDA regulations, specifically 21 C.F.R. Part 110. This introduces ambiguity and contradicts the mandatory nature of GMP regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 110 - Current Good Manufacturing Practice in Manufacturing, Packing, or Holding Human Food
perturbation.law_explanation: The FDA's Current Good Manufacturing Practice (CGMP) regulations in 21 CFR Part 110 are not optional guidelines but mandatory requirements for food manufacturers. The altered text suggests compliance is not mandatory, directly contradicting the regulation that dictates specific practices that 'must' be followed.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates compliance with laws and regulations, including FDA regulations. The changed contract weakens this obligation by stating that Heritage and Jasper 'will endeavor to comply' and products 'may be manufactured' in accordance with regulations. This directly contradicts the mandatory nature of CGMP as explained in the Law Explanation, which states that 21 CFR Part 110 represents mandatory requirements. Even though the snippets do not explicitly say that it 'must' be followed, the Law Explanation does. The change introduces ambiguity about whether full compliance is required.

perturbation.original_text: Premier shall have the sole right, exercisable in its discretion, to initiate and direct the content and scope of a recall, market withdrawal, stock recovery, product correction and/or advisory safety communication (any one or more referred to as a "Recall Action") regarding the Products. At Premier's option, Premier can direct Heritage to, and upon such direction Heritage shall, conduct such Recall Action (and Heritage shall ensure Jasper's cooperation). Premier shall determine, in its sole discretion, the manner, text and timing of any publicity to be given such matters upon prior consultation with Heritage.
perturbation.changed_text: Premier shall have the primary right, exercisable in its discretion, to suggest the content and scope of a recall, market withdrawal, stock recovery, product correction and/or advisory safety communication (any one or more referred to as a "Recall Action") regarding the Products. At Premier's option, Premier can suggest Heritage to, and upon such suggestion Heritage may, conduct such Recall Action (and Heritage shall consider Jasper's cooperation). Premier shall consider the manner, text and timing of any publicity to be given such matters after consultation with Heritage.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'sole right' to 'primary right,' 'direct' to 'suggest,' 'shall conduct' to 'may conduct,' and 'determine' to 'consider' weakens Premier's authority over product recalls. This introduces ambiguity about who ultimately decides on and executes a recall, conflicting with FDA regulations and guidance on recall responsibilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 7.40 outlines the FDA's expectation for a recall strategy, including who is responsible for initiating and executing the recall. The modified text weakens the manufacturer (Heritage)'s obligation to comply with the recalling firm's (Premier)'s directions, which complicates and potentially jeopardizes a timely and effective recall, conflicting with the regulation.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens Premier's authority over product recalls by changing 'sole right' to 'primary right,' 'direct' to 'suggest,' 'shall conduct' to 'may conduct,' and 'determine' to 'consider.' This misalignment of terminology introduces ambiguity about recall responsibilities. The Law Explanation points to 21 CFR § 7.40, which outlines FDA expectations for recall strategy, including responsibility for initiating and executing recalls. While the scraped snippets don't directly quote this section, they reference the CFR Title 21, indicating FDA's regulatory oversight of recalls. This weakening of authority contradicts the legal expectation of clear responsibility, creating a legal contradiction under the Misaligned Terminology definition, making the contract potentially unenforceable or non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: Heritage shall bear all costs, fees and out-of-pocket expenses associated with any Recall Action which results from (i) Heritage's or Jasper's negligence or willful misconduct, (ii) Heritage's or Jasper's failure to comply with Product Specifications or the Post Holdings Quality Expectations Manual set forth on Schedule A, (iii) any breach of this Agreement by Heritage or (iv) ingredients or packaging materials purchased by Heritage or Jasper. In all other cases, Premier shall bear all costs associated with any Recall Action.
perturbation.changed_text: Heritage shall endeavor to bear costs, fees and out-of-pocket expenses associated with any Recall Action which arguably results from (i) Heritage's or Jasper's possible negligence or potential misconduct, (ii) Heritage's or Jasper's alleged failure to comply with Product Specifications or the Post Holdings Quality Expectations Manual set forth on Schedule A, (iii) any perceived breach of this Agreement by Heritage or (iv) ingredients or packaging materials allegedly purchased by Heritage or Jasper. In all other cases, Premier shall bear some costs associated with any Recall Action.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall bear' to 'shall endeavor to bear,' 'results from' to 'arguably results from,' adding qualifiers like 'possible,' 'potential,' and 'alleged' significantly weakens the financial responsibility of Heritage in the event of a recall. Shifting this financial responsibility creates ambiguity and contradicts established legal precedents and FDA guidelines concerning liability in product recall scenarios.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code § 350a - Adulterated infant formula; notification requirements; exemptions; civil penalties
perturbation.law_explanation: While 21 U.S. Code § 350a specifically addresses infant formula recalls, the underlying principles concerning adulteration and responsibility apply broadly to food products. This section implies a clear line of responsibility when adulteration (or, by extension, a cause for recall) stems from a manufacturer's actions (negligence, non-compliance). By introducing ambiguity and weaking Heritage's financial responsibility, the modified text undermines this principle and could impede prompt and effective recall actions, particularly if financial disputes arise.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 21 U.S. Code § 350a - Infant formulas | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 21 U.S. Code § 350a - Infant formulas U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Adulteration An infant formula , including an infant formula powder, shall be deemed to be adulterated if— (1) such infant formula does not provide nutrients as required by subsection (i), (2) such infant formula does not meet the quality factor requirements prescribed by the Secretary under subsection (b)(1), or (3) the processing of such infant formula is not in compliance with the good manufacturing practices and the quality control procedures prescribed by the Secretary under subsection (b)(2). (b) Requirements for quality factors, good manufacturing practices, and retention of records (1) The Secretary shall by regulation establish requirements for quality factors for infant formulas to the extent possible consistent with current scientific knowledge , including quality factor requirements for the nutrients required by subsection (i). (2) (A) The Secretary shall by regulation establish good manufacturing practices for infant formulas , including quality control procedures that the Secretary determines are necessary to assure that an infant formula provides nutrients in accordance with this subsection and subsection (i) and is manufactured in a manner designed to prevent adulteration of the infant formula . (B) The good manufacturing practices and quality control procedures prescribed by the Secretary under subparagraph (A) shall include requirements for— (i) the testing, in accordance with paragraph (3) and by the manufacturer of an infant formula or an agent of such manufacturer, of each batch of infant formula for each nutrient required by subsection (i) before the distribution of such batch, (ii) regularly scheduled testing, by the manufacturer of an infant formula or an agent of such manuf
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens Heritage's financial responsibility in the event of a recall by changing 'shall bear' to 'shall endeavor to bear' and introducing qualifiers like 'possible,' 'potential,' and 'alleged.' This creates ambiguity regarding their obligation. While the scraped law snippets focus on adulteration of infant formulas under 21 U.S. Code § 350a, they highlight the principle that manufacturers are responsible for ensuring product safety and compliance. The original text clearly assigned responsibility based on specific triggers, while the changed text introduces uncertainty. This undermines the clear line of responsibility implied by the need to prevent adulteration and ensure product safety, as emphasized in the law snippets.
